title: Power and Coal
subtitle: Coal-fired generation: operating role, pressure points, and transition choices

slides.title: Power and Coal
slides.subtitle: Coal-fired generation: operating role, pressure points, and transition choices

slides.title: Why coal still matters in some grids
slides.subtitle: Its operating role can outlast its investment appeal
bullets: Coal units can deliver bulk generation on demand, with fuel stored on site rather than arriving in real time.
Existing plants, mines, rail links, and transmission corridors create economic inertia around the installed base.
Where gas, hydro, or storage options are limited, coal can remain a reliability hedge during stressed periods.
slides.footer: System value depends on plant age, fuel logistics, and available replacements

slides.title: How coal becomes electricity
slides.subtitle: Fuel handling, steam cycle, and plant balance must stay coordinated

cards.title: Fuel Handling
cards.body: Coal is delivered, stored, crushed, and fed into the boiler at a controlled rate.

cards.title: Steam Cycle
cards.body: Combustion heats water into steam that spins the turbine and generator set.

cards.title: Plant Balance
cards.body: Cooling, controls, and emissions equipment must all stay in step to keep the unit online.

slides.footer: Performance depends on fuel quality, maintenance discipline, and thermal efficiency

slides.title: Why coal is under pressure
slides.subtitle: The burden is cumulative, not singular

quadrants.title: Cost Stack
quadrants.body: Older units carry heavier maintenance, outage risk, and heat-rate penalties than newer alternatives.

quadrants.title: Environmental Burden
quadrants.body: Ash, sulfur, nitrogen oxides, mercury, and carbon all add control, monitoring, and disposal cost.

quadrants.title: Flexibility Gap
quadrants.body: Large steam units ramp slower and lose efficiency when cycled to follow variable renewables.

quadrants.title: Capital Access
quadrants.body: Capital and policy support increasingly favor cleaner replacement assets over long-life coal retrofits.

slides.footer: The challenge is rarely one issue alone; it is the combined operating burden

slides.title: Transition usually moves in stages
slides.subtitle: The path from dependence to replacement is operational

milestones.title: Stabilize Critical Units
milestones.body: Protect availability, secure fuel supply, and focus maintenance on plants the grid still depends on.

milestones.title: Improve Flexibility
milestones.body: Lower minimum stable load, tighten controls, and reduce avoidable emissions where the units must stay.

milestones.title: Build Replacement Capacity
milestones.body: Add cleaner firm capacity, storage, transmission, and demand flexibility to absorb coal's system role.

milestones.title: Retire or Repurpose Sites
milestones.body: Use existing interconnections, land, and workforce as transition assets instead of abandoning them.

slides.footer: Sequence varies by market design, policy, and what replacement options are actually buildable

slides.title: Replace the function, not the plant
slides.subtitle: Coal exits cleanly only when its system duties move first
bullets: Start from the service the unit provides: energy, capacity, reserves, inertia, voltage support, or local support.
Decide which services are cheaper to preserve, retrofit, or replace with a cleaner resource mix.
Retire plants only after replacement capacity, fuel security, and grid upgrades are credible in operations.
slides.footer: Pragmatic transitions move system duties first and plant closures second
